title: CLI: empty `podman` command isn't helpful or encouraging
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

If someone is starting out with Podman, they might well run `podman` without any additional commands. Currently the response from the CLI is blunt and unhelpful; it doesn't make you want to use the tool.

For comparison, `git` shows the output of `git --help` in this situation. `oc` gives you a helpful description of what it is, and some pointers on how to get started.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Run `podman`.

**Describe the results you received:**

CLI returns;

```
Error: missing command 'podman COMMAND'
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Details to help me get started.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.0.1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.8
Built:        Fri Feb 19 16:56:17 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes, no.

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @allanday!

I like the idea of displaying `podman --help` directly. @baude @mheon WDYT?
this has been a sore spot for me ... but iirc @rhatdan has objections to it. Maybe he has since changed his mind.

I would be fine with a naked command doing this, but the problem is with actual errors like 

podman foobar

And this throws a wall of help information at you hiding that foobar is not a valid command.

BTW Most of coreutils work just like podman.

```
$ podman
Error: missing command 'podman COMMAND'
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
```
Tells the user pretty much what is required.

As opposed to:

```

      --identity string            path to SSH identity file, (CONTAINER_SSHKEY)
      --log-level string           Log messages above specified level (debug, info, warn, warning, error, fatal, panic) (default "warn")
      --namespace string           Set the libpod namespace, used to create separate views of the containers and pods on the system

      --runroot string             Path to the 'run directory' where all state information is stored

      --storage-driver string      Select which storage driver is used to manage storage of images and containers (default is overlay)
      --storage-opt stringArray    Used to pass an option to the storage driver
      --syslog                     Output logging information to syslog as well as the console (default false)
      --tmpdir string              Path to the tmp directory for libpod state content.

```

Which scrolls the error right off the top of the screen.
```
 $ grep
Usage: grep [OPTION]... PATTERNS [FILE]...
Try 'grep --help' for more information.
$ cp
cp: missing file operand
Try 'cp --help' for more information.
$ rm
rm: missing operand
Try 'rm --help' for more information.
...
```

BTW Docker does show help on a naked docker, and shows the nice errors I like on bad commands.

```
$ docker

Usage:  docker [OPTIONS] COMMAND

A self-sufficient runtime for containers

Options:
      --config string      Location of client config files (default "/home/dwalsh/.docker")

  -D, --debug              Enable debug mode

      --tlscert string     Path to TLS certificate file (default "/home/dwalsh/.docker/cert.pem")
      --tlskey string      Path to TLS key file (default "/home/dwalsh/.docker/key.pem")
      --tlsverify          Use TLS and verify the remote
  -v, --version            Print version information and quit

Management Commands:
  app*        Docker App (Docker Inc., v0.9.1-beta3)

Commands:
  attach      Attach local standard input, output, and error streams to a running container
  build       Build an image from a Dockerfile
  commit      Create a new image from a container's changes
  cp          Copy files/folders between a container and the local filesystem
  create      Create a new container
  diff        Inspect changes to files or directories on a container's filesystem
  events      Get real time events from the server
  exec        Run a command in a running container
  export      Export a container's filesystem as a tar archive
  history     Show the history of an image

  import      Import the contents from a tarball to create a filesystem image
  info        Display system-wide information
  inspect     Return low-level information on Docker objects
  kill        Kill one or more running containers
  load        Load an image from a tar archive or STDIN

  logout      Log out from a Docker registry

  pause       Pause all processes within one or more containers
  port        List port mappings or a specific mapping for the container
  ps          List containers

  push        Push an image or a repository to a registry
  rename      Rename a container

  tag         Create a tag TARGET_IMAGE that refers to SOURCE_IMAGE

  unpause     Unpause all processes within one or more containers

Run 'docker COMMAND --help' for more information on a command.

To get more help with docker, check out our guides at https://docs.docker.com/go/guides/
```
And
```
$ docker foobar
docker: 'foobar' is not a docker command.
See 'docker --help'
```

For the naked single command, it's worth considering what `oc` does:

```
$ oc
OpenShift Client 

This client helps you develop, build, deploy, and run your applications on any OpenShift or Kubernetes compatible platform. It also includes the administrative commands for managing a cluster under the 'adm' subcommand.

To create a new application, login to your server and then run new-app: 

  oc login https://mycluster.mycompany.com
  oc new-app centos/ruby-25-centos7~https://github.com/sclorg/ruby-ex.git
  oc logs -f bc/ruby-ex
  
This will create an application based on the Docker image 'centos/ruby-25-centos7' that builds the source code from GitHub. A build will start automatically, push the resulting image to the registry, and a deployment will roll that change out in your project. 

Once your application is deployed, use the status, describe, and get commands to see more about the created components: 

  oc status
  oc describe deploymentconfig ruby-ex
  oc get pods
  
To make this application visible outside of the cluster, use the expose command on the service we just created to create a 'route' (which will connect your application over the HTTP port to a public domain name). 

  oc expose svc/ruby-ex
  oc status
  
You should now see the URL the application can be reached at. 

To see the full list of commands supported, run 'oc --help'
```

This is clearly focused on initial user onboarding, which is what I was primarily interested in for this issue: if we want to funnel people into using Podman, then providing help and pointers is a good idea.

From what I can tell, there are four potential options for a naked `podman` command:

 1. Current behaviour - throw an error, point to `--help`
 2. Show the output of `--help`
 3. Show an abbreviated version of `--help`, with common commands and a suggestion to use `--help` for more
 4. Show a short intro to `podman`, similar to `oc`

In my view 2-4 would be better than the current response, from the perspective of new user onboarding. If there's a concern about the length of `--help` (which I get), then 3 or 4 could be alternatives.

I hadn't intended this issue to apply to the unrecognised command case, but if we are going to consider that, then git's approach of making a suggestion seems like a good one:

```
$ git rebras
git: 'rebras' is not a git command. See 'git --help'.

The most similar command is
	rebase
```

I've certainly found this to be helpful in the past.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Interested in opening a PR?
This would seem to be a good first issue.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I think showing the entire help command on naked podman would be a bit too much. I like using --help instead of getting my command line get filled with help info on running the naked command, because I don't get lost in case I accidentally write `podman` instead of a command like `podman images`
A brief overview could be useful for someone. But I am not completely sure.
The Git like suggestions are really nice to be honest. I actually got them working:
```
[mehul@fedora bin]$ ./podman comit
Error: unrecognized command `podman comit`
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
 Did you mean: 
         [commit commit]
```         
This meant enabling DisableSuggestions field in the rootcmd struct and adding suggestions in the suggestFor field of the commands

If this is wanted then, should I move forward with a PR? WDYT @rhatdan

I think I overthought this, there is literally nothing to be changed except a small addition
This works fine now:
```
[mehul@fedora bin]$ ./podman attack
Error: unrecognized command `podman attack`
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
 Did you mean:
         attach
[mehul@fedora bin]$ ./podman some
Error: unrecognized command `podman some`
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
 Did you mean:
         save
[mehul@fedora bin]$ ./podman ok
Error: unrecognized command `podman ok`
Try 'podman --help' for more information.
 Did you mean:
        cp
        ps
        rm
        top
        pod
```
You planning to make a PR?  I don't know if we have code smart enough to figure out a spelling mistake.  Are you suggesting we just throw out the name of all commands under Did you mean?

@rhatdan I meant listing the relevant commands that could point someone in the right direction in case they forgot the correct name. Cobra handles this for us. It picks the relevant commands, pretty much like git. And this is also used by the kubectl cli too
Docs:
https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/spf13/cobra#readme-suggestions-when-unknown-command-happens
https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/spf13/cobra#Command.SuggestionsFor
The `Command` struct has related fields

Let me know what you think and I'll make changes accordingly

SGTM.  I am all for making this easier for the user.  What I don't want is `podman -help` being puked at the user every time they have a typo.  As long as it is only a few helpful commands, then it sounds great.  (And it is not a lot of engineering work.)
